name: Acyl Halide
description: A carbonyl group bonded to a halogen atom, making it a highly reactive electrophile used in acylation reactions.

name: Aldehyde
description: A carbonyl group (C=O) located at the terminal carbon of a chain, making it more reactive toward nucleophiles than ketones.

name: Allyl
description: A substituent or functional group consisting of a methylene group adjacent to a vinyl group (CH₂=CH-CH₂-), conferring allylic reactivity.

name: Amide
description: A carbonyl group bonded to a nitrogen atom; the most stable of the acyl derivatives due to resonance delocalization of the nitrogen lone pair into the carbonyl.

name: Amino
description: A nitrogen atom bonded to one or more hydrogen atoms (primary amine), making the molecule basic and capable of acting as a nucleophile.

name: Anhydride
description: Two acyl groups connected by an oxygen atom, formed by the condensation of two carboxylic acids with loss of water; a reactive acylating agent.

name: Aromatic Ring (Benzene Ring)
description: A planar cyclic ring of six sp2-hybridized carbons with delocalized pi electrons, conferring exceptional stability and characteristic electrophilic aromatic substitution reactivity.

name: Carboxyl
description: A carbonyl group bonded to a hydroxyl group at the same carbon, forming a carboxylic acid that readily donates its proton.

name: Disulfide
description: A linkage between two sulfur atoms, formed by the oxidation of two thiol groups; critically important in stabilizing the tertiary structure of proteins.

name: Enol
description: A tautomeric form of carbonyl compounds in which the alpha hydrogen migrates to the oxygen, producing a hydroxyl-substituted alkene; typically in equilibrium with the keto form.

name: Epoxide
description: A cyclic ether containing a three-membered ring with one oxygen atom; ring strain makes it highly reactive toward nucleophilic ring-opening.
properties: opens with nucleophiles under acid or base conditions

name: Ester
description: A carbonyl group bonded to an alkoxy group (-OR'), formed by condensation of a carboxylic acid and an alcohol; characteristic of fats, oils, and many fragrances.
properties: less reactive than acyl halides or anhydrides

name: Ether
description: An oxygen atom bonded to two carbon groups; relatively unreactive under normal conditions due to the absence of a labile hydrogen, making ethers useful as solvents.
properties: forms explosive peroxides on prolonged air exposure

name: Halide (Alkyl Halide)
description: A carbon atom bonded to a halogen atom (fluorine, chlorine, bromine, or iodine), making the carbon electrophilic and susceptible to nucleophilic substitution or elimination.
properties: reactivity order: I > Br > Cl > F

name: Hydroxyl
description: A polar group consisting of an oxygen-hydrogen bond attached to carbon, making molecules water-soluble and capable of forming hydrogen bonds.

name: Imine
description: A nitrogen-carbon double bond (C=N), analogous to a carbonyl but with nitrogen; formed by condensation of a carbonyl compound with a primary amine, also called a Schiff base.
properties: hydrolyzable back to carbonyl and amine
found in retinal-opsin linkage in vision

name: Ketone
description: A carbonyl group (C=O) flanked by two carbon substituents, less reactive than aldehydes but important in metabolism and synthesis.
properties: cannot be oxidized further without C-C cleavage

name: Lactam
description: A cyclic amide in which the amide nitrogen and carbonyl are part of a ring; beta-lactams form the core of penicillin and cephalosporin antibiotics.

name: Lactone
description: A cyclic ester formed by intramolecular condensation of a hydroxy acid; found widely in natural products including macrolide antibiotics and flavor compounds.

name: Nitro
description: A nitrogen atom bonded to two oxygens (one via double bond, one via single bond with negative formal charge), strongly electron-withdrawing and reducible to an amino group.
properties: deactivates aromatic ring toward electrophilic substitution

name: Nitrile
description: A carbon atom triple-bonded to nitrogen, making the carbon electrophilic and the group hydrolyzable to a carboxylic acid or reducible to an amine.
properties: hydrolyzable to amide then carboxylic acid

name: Phenol
description: A hydroxyl group attached directly to an aromatic ring, which stabilizes the negative charge of the conjugate base through resonance, making phenols much more acidic than alcohols.

name: Phosphate
description: A phosphorus atom bonded to four oxygens (one or more as ester linkages); the backbone of DNA and RNA and a key energy carrier in ATP.

name: Sulfonate
description: A sulfur atom bonded to three oxygens and one carbon; sulfonic acids are much stronger acids than carboxylic acids and sulfonates are excellent leaving groups.

name: Thioether (Sulfide)
description: A sulfur atom bonded to two carbon groups, the sulfur analog of an ether; less polar than ethers and more nucleophilic due to sulfur's large polarizable electron cloud.

name: Thiol
description: A sulfur atom bonded to hydrogen, the sulfur analog of a hydroxyl group; more acidic and nucleophilic than alcohols due to sulfur's larger atomic radius.

name: Alkene (Vinyl group in isolation)
description: A carbon-carbon double bond consisting of a sigma bond and a pi bond; the pi electrons are nucleophilic and reactive toward electrophilic addition reactions.

name: Alkyne
description: A carbon-carbon triple bond consisting of one sigma and two pi bonds; terminal alkynes are weakly acidic and the triple bond is reactive toward electrophilic and nucleophilic addition.

name: Vinyl
description: An ethenyl substituent group (CH₂=CH-) attached to a molecule; vinyl groups are reactive in polymerization and cross-coupling reactions.
properties: reactive toward radical and ionic polymerization
electrophilic when attached to electron-withdrawing groups

name: Carbonate Ester
description: An ester of carbonic acid featuring two alkoxy groups flanking a carbonyl; used as solvents, in polycarbonate plastics, and as reagents for introducing carbonyl groups.
